era_context: The late 16th century: the Spanish Habsburg Empire at its height; the Reformation and Counter-Reformation reshaping European religious boundaries; the Ottoman Empire consolidating Mediterranean power; the Ming Dynasty in China under the Jiajing Emperor; the Mughal Empire under Akbar expanding in South Asia; early European colonial ventures in the Americas and Asia.

events.title: Beeldenstorm (Iconoclastic Fury) in the Spanish Netherlands
events.description: The Beeldenstorm, or Iconoclastic Fury, swept through the Spanish Netherlands as Protestant mobs destroyed religious images and artwork in Catholic churches. This wave of iconoclasm was triggered by religious tensions, the spread of Calvinist ideas, and opposition to Spanish Catholic rule. The Beeldenstorm marked a turning point in the religious conflict in the Netherlands and contributed to the escalation of tensions that would lead to the Dutch Revolt in 1568.

sources.name: Spanish and Dutch chronicles and administrative records
Contemporary accounts by Spanish and Dutch historians

events.certainty_note: The Beeldenstorm is precisely dated and extensively documented in Spanish and Dutch sources.

events.title: Suleiman the Magnificent's Death (Ottoman Empire)
events.description: Suleiman the Magnificent, one of the greatest Ottoman sultans, died after a 46-year reign (1520–1566). He was succeeded by his son Selim II. Suleiman's reign had seen the Ottoman Empire reach its territorial and cultural zenith, with major military campaigns, architectural achievements, and administrative reforms. His death marked the beginning of a gradual shift in Ottoman power dynamics, though the empire remained formidable.
events.certainty_note: Suleiman's death is precisely dated in Ottoman records.

events.title: Establishment of the Spanish Inquisition in Mexico
events.description: The Spanish Inquisition was formally established in Mexico (New Spain) to enforce Catholic orthodoxy and suppress indigenous religious practices and heresy. This marked the beginning of systematic religious conversion and cultural transformation of Mexico under Spanish colonial rule. The Inquisition would remain active in Mexico until the early 19th century.

sources.name: Spanish colonial administrative records and Inquisitorial documents

events.certainty_note: The formal establishment of the Inquisition in Mexico is documented in Spanish colonial records, though the exact date may reflect administrative formalization rather than the beginning of religious enforcement.

events.title: Battle of Talikota (Deccan, India)
events.description: The Battle of Talikota (also known as the Battle of Rakshasa-Tangadi) was fought between the Vijayanagara Empire and an alliance of Deccan sultanates. The Vijayanagara forces were defeated, marking a significant decline in Vijayanagara power and influence in South India. This battle had major consequences for the political balance in South India and contributed to the rise of the Deccan sultanates.
key_figures: Deccan sultanate forces (Bijapur, Golconda, Ahmadnagar, Bahmani Sultanate)

sources.name: South Indian chronicles and administrative records
Later compilations of South Indian history

events.certainty_note: The Battle of Talikota is documented in South Indian sources, though some details are disputed among historians.

events.title: Akbar's Consolidation of Mughal Power
events.description: The Mughal Emperor Akbar continued consolidating his power and expanding Mughal territory in South Asia. In 1566, Akbar was in the midst of his military campaigns and administrative reforms that would establish the Mughal Empire as one of the world's largest and most powerful states. This period marked the height of Mughal expansion and the development of Akbar's syncretic religious policies.

sources.name: Mughal administrative records and the Akbarnama by Abul Fazl

events.certainty_note: This entry describes a general context of Mughal consolidation rather than a specific event in 1566.

disconfirming_evidence: The Beeldenstorm is sometimes portrayed as a spontaneous popular uprising, but modern historians emphasize the role of organized Calvinist activists and the complexity of motivations beyond simple religious fervor. Suleiman the Magnificent is often idealized in Western historiography; modern scholarship emphasizes the challenges he faced in his later years and the limitations of Ottoman power even at its height. The Battle of Talikota is sometimes presented as a decisive end to Vijayanagara power, but the empire continued to exist and exercise influence for another century, though in a diminished capacity.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1566 is rich in European sources, particularly Spanish, Dutch, and Ottoman records. South Indian sources exist but are less frequently integrated into Western historical narratives. Mughal sources are detailed but less commonly cited in English-language historiography. The perspective is heavily weighted toward European and Ottoman political and military events. Sub-Saharan African, Pacific Islander, and Central Asian perspectives are almost entirely absent from surviving written records for this year. The sources themselves reflect the biases of their creators: Spanish records justify Catholic orthodoxy; Dutch sources emphasize religious freedom; Ottoman records emphasize imperial authority; South Indian sources reflect regional political interests.

graph_edges.note: The Beeldenstorm in 1566 contributed to the escalation of tensions that led to the Dutch Revolt in 1568.
Suleiman's death in 1566 occurred before the Battle of Lepanto in 1571, which would be fought under his successor Selim II.
The Battle of Talikota in 1566 marked the beginning of Vijayanagara's decline as a major South Indian power.
